vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits or adds a sysctl configuration line and applies the setting at runtime. This change does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Information leakage via exposed kernel pointers; Potential for local privilege escalation if pointers are disclosed

execution_details.stdout:
# when using 1 interface for two networks when using NETKEY, the kernel
# thinks it can be clever by sending a redirect (cause it cannot tell
# an encrypted packet came in, but a decrypted packet came out),
# so it sends a bogus ICMP redirect
#
# We disable redirects for XFRM/IPsec
# net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
# net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0

user.max_user_namespaces = 0

execution_details.stdout:
# sysctl settings are defined through files in
# /usr/lib/sysctl.d/, /run/sysctl.d/, and /etc/sysctl.d/.
#
# Vendors settings live in /usr/lib/sysctl.d/.
# To override a whole file, create a new file with the same in
# /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there. To override
# only specific settings, add a file with a lexically later
# name in /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there.
#
# For more information, see sysctl.conf(5) and sysctl.d(5).
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf

# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians = 1
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.default.log_martians = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.default.log_martians = 1
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1

net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1

net.ipv4.icmp_ignore_bogus_error_responses = 1
# Per : Set net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
# Per : Set net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects = 0
# Per : Set kernel.core_pattern = |/bin/false in /etc/sysctl.conf
kernel.core_pattern = |/bin/false
# Per : Set kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1
# Per : Set kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf
kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1
# Per : Set kernel.perf_event_paranoid = 2 in /etc/sysctl.conf
kernel.perf_event_paranoid = 2
# Per : Set kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled = 1 in /etc/sysctl.conf

kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 1

net.core.bpf_jit_harden = 2
# Per : Set kernel.randomize_va_space = 2 in /etc/sysctl.conf

kernel.randomize_va_space = 0
kernel.kptr_restrict = 0

net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1
kernel.dmesg_restrict = 0

llm_verdict.message:
I’m glad the remediation went smoothly and the system is now compliant with **sysctl_kernel_kptr_restrict**.  

If you have any additional OpenSCAP findings, configuration checks, or other security‑hardening tasks you’d like to tackle, just let me know. I can:

* Scan for other sysctl settings that need adjustment  
* Verify the current state of kernel parameters, file permissions, services, etc.  
* Apply targeted remediations (e.g., FS.protected_* settings, audit rules, SELinux policies, password policies, firewall rules)  

Feel free to share any other findings or let me know which area you’d like to review next!

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to remediate sysctl kernel.kptr_restrict. Need to set to 1 (restrict pointers). Usually recommended value is 1 or 2. Checking SSG: for kernel.kptr_restrict, secure value is 1. We'll read existing sysctl config files for any occurrences and modify. Plan: 1) Check /etc/sysctl.conf and /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf for lines containing kernel.kptr_restrict. 2) If found, ensure value set to 1, uncomment if needed. Use sed to replace. 3) If not found, create file /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf with line "kernel.kptr_restrict = 1". 4) Apply runtime using sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=1.

We must review plan via review_plan.

Proceed.

We have approval. Need to implement steps. Must read files first before modification. We'll inspect /etc/sysctl.conf and /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf etc. Use read_file for each.

We need to check if any file has non-commented kernel.kptr_restrict. We'll read list of files. We can using run_cmd to list files glob. Use run_cmd with "ls /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null". Then read each. Then decide.

Implement: read /etc/sysctl.conf first. Then list files, iterate.

We'll need to use python logic but we are just sequential. We'll read files, check content for lines matching non-commented kernel.kptr_restrict. We can use run_cmd grep but cannot risk editing wrong. But we need to decide programmatically.

Simplify: We'll just create new file /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf with setting 1. But the plan said modify existing if present to avoid duplicate. But adding new file won't cause duplicate as duplicate lines are allowed? Could cause two entries, last wins? Could cause warnings. Safer to check existing. Let's read files and see if any line contains kernel.kptr_restrict (not commented). We'll attempt to find via grep.

We can run "grep -R -E '^[[:space:]]*kernel\\.kptr_restrict' /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null". Then parse. But we need to avoid modifying if no file. Let's just read each file and check.

We'll read /etc/sysctl.conf content. Then list files.

Proceed:

We tried listing with globs, but exit_code 2 because some patterns not found? Actually the shell returned error for missing files? It used ls with multiple patterns; if any pattern fails, exit code non-zero. But we got output of existing files, plus warning in stderr (maybe from some other program). We have list of files: /etc/sysctl.d/50-libreswan.conf, 99-disable-user-namespaces.conf, 99-sysctl.conf.

We need to inspect each for kernel.kptr_restrict. Let's read each.
We need to check other files for kernel.kptr_restrict. Let's read /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-user-namespaces.conf and 99-sysctl.conf.
We need to check other files: /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf and maybe /etc/sysctl.conf.

We need to remediate kernel.kptr_restrict. The file /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf already contains lines:

kernel.kptr_restrict = 0

We need to set to 1. Also there are duplicate lines of kernel.kptr_restrict maybe only that one. Want to modify in-place.

Plan: Use sed -i to replace line matching '^\\s*kernel\\.kptr_restrict\\s*=.*$' to 'kernel.kptr_restrict = 1' in /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf.

Then apply runtime sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=1.

Potential need to reload sysctl --system to ensure config loaded, but runtime set already does. We'll also run sysctl --system to ensure persistence.

But we must not violate rules: use one command at a time. So first modify file via run_cmd using sed -i.

Before that, read file (done). Then run sed.

After modification, read file again to confirm.

Then apply runtime.

Then optionally run sysctl --system (but maybe just run sysctl -w then sysctl -p? Better to run sysctl --system to load all files; single command.

Proceed.

We have modified the line to set = 1. Need to ensure runtime applied. Also need to reload sysctl. Use sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=1.

Proceed.
The user says remediation is complete; we should respond acknowledging.
reasoning_messages.content: Great! The `kernel.kptr_restrict` parameter is now set to **1** in `/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf` and has been applied at runtime. This brings the system into compliance with the OpenSCAP rule **sysctl_kernel_kptr_restrict** and ensures the setting persists across reboots. Let me know if there’s anything else you’d like to address.

reasoning_messages.reasoning: The user just acknowledges. Probably respond that nothing else needed.

I’m glad the remediation went smoothly and the system is now compliant with **sysctl_kernel_kptr_restrict**.  

If you have any additional OpenSCAP findings, configuration checks, or other security‑hardening tasks you’d like to tackle, just let me know. I can:

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly sets kernel.kptr_restrict to 1, updates or creates a sysctl config file, and applies the change at runtime, which will remediate the vulnerability.
concerns: The sed pattern may match unintended lines if not anchored, potentially corrupting other sysctl settings.
The plan mentions reloading sysctl configurations as optional; without a reload the new file may not be applied until next reboot.
suggested_improvements: Anchor the sed replacement to the exact key (e.g., '^\s*kernel\.kptr_restrict\s*=.*$') to avoid accidental edits.
Execute sysctl --system (or sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf) after creating or modifying the file to ensure the setting is loaded immediately.
Consider using a tool like 'sysctl -w' followed by 'sysctl -p' rather than editing files directly for simpler idempotent handling.
